content.text: JavaScript provides four static methods on
for coordinating multiple asynchronous operations at once:

. Each accepts an iterable of Promises and returns a new Promise that settles based on a different rule. Picking the right combinator is the difference between needless serial latency and efficient parallel execution — and between masked failures and properly surfaced errors.
All four combinators take any iterable (array, Set, etc.) of Promises and return a single Promise that reflects the collective outcome. They differ in
— resolves when every Promise fulfills; rejects immediately if any one rejects.
— settles (resolve or reject) with whichever Promise settles first.
— resolves once every Promise has settled (regardless of outcome); never rejects.
— resolves with the first fulfilled Promise; rejects only if every Promise rejects.
All four start their input Promises simultaneously — none waits for the previous to complete before starting the next.
Without these combinators, running multiple async operations correctly requires either serial
chains (unnecessarily slow) or complex manual tracking of completion state. Sequentially awaiting two independent 500 ms operations takes 1000 ms;
finishes in 500 ms. At UI scale — a dashboard that fetches user data, notifications, and recent activity — the difference is visible to the user.
Beyond performance, the combinators express intent precisely.
makes it obvious that failures are expected and handled individually.
makes a timeout pattern readable at a glance. Choosing correctly makes the code both faster and easier to reason about.
All input Promises are initiated at once; wall-clock time equals the slowest single operation, not their sum.
The choice of combinator communicates the failure semantics without a comment.
preserves input order regardless of which Promise resolved first;
returns a structured report on every operation.
fail-fast: a single rejection abandons all others. If you need each result independently, use
does not cancel the slower Promises — they continue running in the background, consuming resources, even after the race is settled.
when all inputs reject — a less familiar error type that requires deliberate handling.
Firing many Promises simultaneously can overwhelm a server or rate limiter. For large arrays, batch into chunks rather than calling

Iterates its input and subscribes to each Promise. It maintains an internal counter of fulfilled values. When a Promise fulfills, its value is stored at the corresponding index and the counter increments. When the counter reaches the total input count,
resolves with an array of those values
— not arrival order. If any Promise rejects before that point,
rejects immediately with that rejection reason, discarding all other outcomes.

Subscribes to all Promises and resolves or rejects as soon as the first one settles, passing through that settlement's value or reason. Subsequent settlements from slower Promises are ignored (though those Promises themselves are not cancelled — there is no cancellation mechanism in the Promise API).
but treats rejections the same as fulfillments — it records the outcome and continues counting. Once every input has settled, it resolves with an array of descriptor objects, each with a
field respectively. The outer Promise itself never rejects.
in failure semantics. It resolves with the first fulfillment and ignores all rejections that arrive before that point. Only when
inputs have rejected does it reject — with an
property contains all the individual rejection reasons in input order.

The diagram below shows how all four combinators handle the same three input Promises: P1 resolves first, P2 rejects second, and P3 resolves last. The bold vertical line in each row marks when that combinator settles and with what outcome.

content.text: All results are required; any failure means the whole operation fails
You want the fastest result, or need a timeout
Rejects if the first to settle is a rejection
You need every outcome, success or failure, to act on each individually
You need the first successful result; individual failures are acceptable
Promise.allSettled — bulk operations with partial failure
Promise.any — try multiple sources, use the first that works
Parallel map — transform an array with async work
A React dashboard component that loads three independent data sources in parallel using
so a slow or failing widget does not block the others:
here means a flaky stats endpoint will not blank out the entire dashboard — only the stats widget shows an error while user and posts render normally.
Using Promise.all when failures are recoverable
Forgetting that Promise.race doesn't cancel losers
Calling Promise.all on an empty array
Default to Promise.all for required parallel data.
If all results are needed and any failure should surface,
Use Promise.allSettled when failures are tolerable.
Whenever a subset of results is still useful, use
Always cancel the losing requests to avoid wasted network and server resources.
Guard Promise.any against the empty-array case.
rejects immediately — check for an empty iterable before calling it.
opens 1000 parallel connections. Process in chunks of 5–20 to respect rate limits and server capacity.
The primary performance benefit of all four combinators is parallel execution. For
For high-volume scenarios (sending emails to 10,000 users, processing a large import), running all Promises simultaneously is not safe. Implement a concurrency-limited batch pattern:
holds all resolved values in memory simultaneously. For large arrays of large objects this matters — process incrementally (batch + reduce) rather than resolving everything at once.
inside a loop body — the fix is usually
with a concurrency cap — useful when you need bounded parallelism without hand-rolling the batching loop.
semantics) and provides individual loading/error state for each — the production-grade alternative to raw

Firing many requests simultaneously can trigger server-side rate limiting (HTTP 429). Design batch sizes based on the API's documented rate limit. When rate-limited, implement exponential back-off before retrying.
in security-sensitive flows (e.g. checking multiple authorization services), ensure that a rejection from a required service is treated as a denial, not silently omitted. Always validate that the
In React, always pair parallel fetches with
to prevent state updates on unmounted components and to cancel superseded requests when the component re-renders with new props.
Think of the four combinators as four different race policies at a relay station with multiple incoming runners (Promises):
— "We leave only when everyone has arrived. If anyone drops out, the whole group is cancelled."
— "We leave with whoever arrives first, whether they succeeded or not."
— "We wait for everyone to finish. We write down each person's result — whether they finished or dropped out — and then leave."
— "We leave with the first person who
arrives. Drop-outs don't count unless everyone drops out."
All four combinators start their input Promises simultaneously. They differ only in when they settle and what value or reason they produce.
resolves with all values in input order; rejects on the first rejection. Use it when every result is required.
settles with the first-settling Promise (resolve or reject). Pair with
never rejects; it always produces a per-item outcome report. Use it for optional or independent parallel operations.
resolves with the first fulfillment; rejects with
Batch large arrays into fixed-size chunks to avoid overwhelming rate limits or holding excessive memory simultaneously.
In React, combine these combinators with
to cancel in-flight requests on unmount or prop change.
